title: Polynomial Patterns in Finite Fields: a Dynamical Point of View
speaker: Ethan M Ackelsberg
abstract: In its dynamical formulation, the Furstenberg—Sárközy theorem states that for any invertible measure-preserving system (X,μ,T), any set A⊆X with μ(A) greater than 0, and any integer polynomial P with P(0)=0,   c(A)=limN−M→∞1N−M∑n=MN−1μ(A∩TP(n)A)>0.   The limit c(A) obtains the ``correct'' value μ(A)2 when T is \emph{totally ergodic}. In fact, when T is totally ergodic, one has an ergodic theorem for polynomial actions: for any integer polynomial P and any f∈L2(μ),   limN−M→∞1N−M∑n=MN−1TP(n)f=∫Xf dμ,

proof_stages.label: Finite-field polynomial-pattern target and multilinear reformulation
proof_stages.summary: The talk begins from Sarah Peluse's finite-field theorem counting configurations x, x+P_1(y), ..., x+P_m(y) for linearly independent integer polynomials, then reformulates the counting statement as a stronger multilinear average with product-of-means main term and power-saving error.

proof_stages.label: Characteristic-zero ergodic heuristic
proof_stages.summary: The finite-field multilinear estimate is compared heuristically with the Bergelson-Leibman type theorem that polynomial multiple ergodic averages in totally ergodic systems converge to the product of integrals.

proof_stages.label: Total ergodicity as absence of periodic obstructions
proof_stages.summary: Total ergodicity for a Z-action is recast through equivalent forms: all powers are ergodic, actions along finite-index ideals are ergodic, there are no nontrivial periodic factors, and polynomial averages behave ergodically.

proof_stages.label: Asymptotic total ergodicity for finite rings
proof_stages.summary: For a finite commutative ring acting on itself by translations, ideals produce periodic factors, leading to the least-periodic-factor invariant and the definition that a sequence of finite rings is asymptotically totally ergodic when this invariant tends to infinity.

proof_stages.label: Fixed-characteristic replacement by F_p[t]-dynamics
proof_stages.summary: To treat finite fields of fixed characteristic, the limiting infinitary object is changed from Z to the polynomial ring F_p[t], since finite fields of characteristic p are quotients by irreducible polynomials, and total ergodicity is defined for F[t]-actions along finite-index principal ideals.

proof_stages.label: Polynomial averages for totally ergodic F[t]-actions
proof_stages.summary: For a totally ergodic F[t]-action and a polynomial with coefficients in F[t], the single polynomial ergodic average over monic polynomials converges in L^2 not necessarily to the integral but to a projection onto a polynomial-dependent invariant subspace, proved via a spectral reduction and Weyl-type equidistribution.

proof_stages.label: Irrational-distribution criterion for integral limits
proof_stages.summary: Characters of the additive group of F[t] are divided into rational and irrational ones, and a polynomial is declared good for irrational distribution precisely when all irrational character averages vanish, which is the condition forcing the projection in the F[t] theorem to be the constants in every system.

proof_stages.label: Finitary quotient estimates and low-characteristic applications
proof_stages.summary: The infinitary F[t] picture is transferred to quotient rings F[t]/q, giving quantitative polynomial-average estimates controlled by the least prime factor of q and derivational degree, and yielding intersective-polynomial difference bounds, bipartite difference-counting estimates, and a low-characteristic finite-field analogue of Peluse's theorem under an irrational-distribution hypothesis.

examples.name: Modular rings and least prime factor
examples.note: The ring Z/nZ is used to make the least-periodic-factor invariant concrete: its periodic factors correspond to divisors of n, so the invariant is the least prime factor of n.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the least periodic factor for Z/nZ as the least prime factor of n, explaining it via ideals/divisors of n

passes.reason: Z/nZ is an ordinary modular quotient, while the named LPF invariant is the minimum-index/least-prime-factor quantity.

examples.name: Rotation on three points along n^2
examples.note: In answer to a question, a rotation on a three-point system sampled along n^2 illustrates how non-total-ergodicity can yield a limiting operator that is not the expected projection because the polynomial orbit is unbalanced.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker analyzes the n^2 orbit for rotation on three points, giving its unequal limiting proportions of 1/3 and 2/3

examples.name: Classical Weyl equidistribution for irrational polynomial phases
examples.note: The characteristic-zero fact that any nonconstant integer polynomial multiplied by an irrational equidistributes mod 1 is used as a comparison showing why the positive-characteristic irrational-distribution condition is a genuine new restriction.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites the classical Weyl equidistribution fact for irrational polynomial phases without carrying out a computation or proof

passes.reason: Weyl equidistribution for irrational nonconstant polynomial phases is a standard general phenomenon used as background and contrast.

examples.name: Circle-method nonlinear equations with many variables
examples.note: An audience question invokes examples such as linear equations in primes and cubic equations in many variables to contrast the robustness of the circle method with the more separated-variable nature of the dynamical/Cauchy-Schwarz approach discussed in the talk.
